State of Callfomia

At the February 1, 1983, 
ice request meeting, there was a 
it of opinion on whether an As 
who receives two 
t 
kets marked "Assemblyman Smitn and Mrs. Smlth" must r 
rt 
one or both tickets. 
Our past adv 
ind 
tes that we have

JSIV1: km 


State of California 
Memorandum 
To 
Advice Request Meeting Participants 
Date 
Feb. 1, 1983 
From 
: 
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION 
Subject: 
Janis Shank McLean 
9~/fl-/' 
Advice Request from Vic Bellerue, 
Deputy County Counsel 
Facts: 
As a county employee, Mr. Bellerue received 6 
tickets to Disneyland which were earmarked as being for the 
"employee and his family." 
Question: 
Should he report the value of all 6 ticKets or 
just the value of the ticket that he uses himself? 
Discussion: 
In the past, with gifts other than tickets, we 
have not required the gift, if received by a spouse or dependent 
child, to be reported. 
Similarly, in the case of joint gifts, 
the spouse's share is not reportable. 
Torres, 2 FPPC 31: 
Spouses have equal shares of a 
wedding gift and only the filer's share is reportable. 
Cory, 2 FPPC 48: 
Gifts received by a spouse and 
dependent children are generally not reported. 
M-77-492: 
When spouses receive a joint gift of a meal 
or a vacation, only the filer's share is reportable. 
In the case of tickets, the only written advice I found 
(although a lot of oral advice has been given) is in the Hopkins 
Opinion, 3 FPPC 107. 
In that Opinion, a filer recieved a pass to 
Disneyland that allowed 4 persons into the park. 
We required the 
value of all 4 admissions to be reported, although the Opinion 
clearly anticipated that some of the tickets would be used by 
family members. 
(Disneyland had not placed any restrictions on 
the use of the tickets.) 
We have been giving oral advice, based 
upon Hopkins, that tickets received by a filer are reportable, 
even if they are earmarked for a spouse or a child (e.g. 2 gala 
tickets for Senator and Mr s. Jo nes) . 
I do not think that this advice is correct. 
In HopKins, I 
think that the filer was required to report all of the tickets 


Advice Request Meeting Participants 
Feb. 1, 1983 
Page 2 
because the filer could exercise direction and control over the 
use of the tickets, which made them gifts to him. 
In the present 
case, Disneyland has earmarked the tickets for use by the filer's 
family. 
Thus, the tickets are really gifts to those individuals 
and the filer is only a conduit. 
The extra tickets should not be 
reportable by the filer (as in Cory, Torres, etc.) 
Do you agree? 
JSM:plh

sections.question:

just the value of the ticket that he uses himself?
sections.conclusion: Pending further consideration of this matter, our advice is to require the reporting of both tickets.

sections.question_synthetic: Whether a county employee who receives tickets earmarked for the employee and family members must report the value of all tickets or only those for personal use.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC advised that both tickets must be reported when received by an assemblymember and spouse, though the document notes inconsistency in past handling of tickets versus other gifts and seeks clarification on whether tickets earmarked for family members should follow the same rules as other gifts.
sections.parsing_notes: Mixed formats: modern, old; LLM: Document contains two related memoranda from February-March 1983. First memo (M-83-056) briefly states both tickets must be reported. Second memo provides fuller discussion questioning whether past ticket advice (requiring all tickets be reported) is correct, arguing tickets earmarked for family should follow same rules as other gifts. Document appears incomplete or contains working notes rather than final conclusion. Some OCR errors present (e.g., 'Callfomia', 'rch' for 'March'). Confidence lowered due to apparent inconsistency between the two sections and indication that advice is pending further consideration.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Should he report the value of all 6 ticKets or 
just the value of the ticket that he uses himself?

CONCLUSION: Pending further consideration of this matter, our advice is to require the reporting of both tickets.

JSIV1: km State of California Memorandum To Advice Request Meeting Participants Date Feb. 1, 1983 From : FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION Subject: Janis Shank McLean 9~/fl-/' Advice Request from Vic Bellerue, Deputy County Counsel Facts: As a county employee, Mr. Bellerue received 6 tickets to Disneyland which were earmarked as being for the "employee and his family." Question: Should he report the value of all 6 ticKets or just the value of the ticket that he uses himself? Discussion: In the past, with gifts other than tickets, we have not required the gift, if received by a spouse or dependent child, to be reported. Similarly, in the case of joint gifts, the spouse's share is not reportable. Torres, 2 FPPC 31: Spouses have equal shares of a wedding gift and only the filer's share is reportable. Cory, 2 FPPC 48: Gifts received by a spouse and dependent children are generally not reported. M-77-492: When spouses receive a joint gift of a meal or a vacation, only the filer's share is reportable. In the case of tickets, the only written advice I found (although a lot of oral advice has been given) is in the Hopkins Opinion, 3 FPPC 107. In that Opinion, a filer recieved a pass to Disneyland that allowed 4 persons into the park. We required the value of all 4 admissions to be reported, although the Opinion clearly anticipated that some of the tickets would be used by family members. (Disneyland had not placed any restrictions on the use of the tickets.) We have been giving oral advice, based upon Hopkins, that tickets received by a filer are reportable, even if they are earmarked for a spouse or a child (e.g. 2 gala tickets for Senator and Mr s. Jo nes) . I do not think that this advice is correct. In HopKins, I think that the filer was required to report all of the tickets Advice Request Meeting Participants Feb. 1, 1983 Page 2 because the filer could exercise direction and control over the use of the tickets, which made them gifts to him. In the present case, Disneyland has earmarked the tickets for use by the filer's family. Thus, the tickets are really gifts to those individuals and the filer is only a conduit. The extra tickets should not be reportable by the filer (as in Cory, Torres, etc.) Do you agree? JSM:plh
embedding.summary: FPPC advice letter addressing whether gifts of tickets to public officials must be fully reported or only the portion used by the official, with discussion of how tickets differ from other gifts like meals regarding reporting requirements for family members.
